question.text: Weighted average anti-dilution differs from full ratchet in that:

answer.text: It provides no protection to investors
It adjusts the conversion price considering both the old and new round prices weighted by the number of shares
It completely eliminates dilution for existing investors
It is only applicable to listed companies

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Weighted average anti-dilution adjusts the conversion price to a weighted average of the old price and the new (lower) round price, weighted by the number of shares at each price. This results in a less severe adjustment than full ratchet, balancing investor protection with fairness to founders. **The Trap:** There are two variants — broad-based (includes all outstanding shares in the calculation) and narrow-based (only includes preferred shares). Broad-based is more common and more founder-friendly. **Cross-Exam Context:** Anti-dilution mechanisms are detailed in XIX-A and XIX-C. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Broad-based weighted average is the market standard in India. It provides reasonable downside protection while maintaining a healthy relationship between the AIF and the investee company's founders.
